summary: Spolsky argues that computer science programs fail to teach practical software development skills like version control, teamwork, and project management. He highlights UCOSP, a consortium of Canadian universities organizing cross-university capstone projects on open source software. He then diagnoses why student projects consistently fail to deliver useful results: students have 16 years of experience procrastinating and no experience with long-term team schedules. The solution, he argues, is weekly deadlines and milestones, since long-term deadlines without short-term checkpoints are rarely met — a problem that affects professional programmers just as much as students.
key_points: CS departments have become divorced from real-world software development by prioritizing theory over practical skills like version control, teamwork, and debugging
Student capstone projects consistently fail because students have zero experience managing long-term, team-based schedules and default to last-minute cramming
The 'interesting' academic portion of code is only 10% of the work — the other 90% needed to make software survive real users is never taught
Long-term deadlines without short-term milestones are rarely met, whether by students or professional programmers
The key difference between student teams and professional teams isn't discipline — it's that professional teams have managers who set frequent intermediate deadlines
money_quotes: Many universities have managed to convince themselves that the more irrelevant the curriculum is to the real world, the more elite they are.
College students in their final year have about 16 years of experience doing short projects and leaving everything until the last minute.
Until you're a senior in college, you're very unlikely to have ever encountered an assignment that can't be done by staying up all night.

The other 90% of the work that it takes to bring code up to the level of 'useful, real-world code' is never expected from undergrads, because it's not 'interesting' to fix bugs and deal with real-world conditions.
It appears to be a permanent part of the human condition that long term deadlines without short term milestones are rarely met.
Students have exactly zero experience with long term, team-based schedules. Therefore, they almost always do crappy work when given a term-length project and told to manage their time themselves.

The only reason the real world gets this right where all-student college teams fail is because in the real world there are managers, who can set deadlines, which a team of students who are all peers can't pull off.
key_insight: Long-term deadlines without frequent short-term milestones fail universally — for students and professionals alike — and the real value of management is imposing that cadence of regular deliverables.
